Tax Impact Summary
ESOP Sale — Comparative Tax Analysis
Seller
{{sellerName}}
Company
{{companyName}}
CPA
{{cpaName}}
Date
{{sentDate}}
§ 01Purpose

This summary compares the tax treatment of a sale to an ESOP versus a sale to a third-party buyer (strategic or financial). It is prepared for the seller's CPA to review and validate against the seller's specific circumstances.

Disclaimer: Forhemit is not a tax advisor. This summary uses standard assumptions and publicly available tax rates. The seller's CPA should verify all inputs and calculations based on the seller's actual tax situation.
§ 02Transaction Inputs
{{salePrice}}
{{sellerBasis}}
{{capitalGain}}
{{entityType}}
{{sellerState}}
{{filingStatus}}
§ 03Scenario A: Sale to Third-Party Buyer

In a traditional M&A sale, the seller recognizes capital gain in the year of closing.

ItemAmount
Gross sale proceeds{{salePrice}}
Less: seller's basis({{sellerBasis}})
Capital gain{{capitalGain}}
Federal LTCG tax (20%){{federalLTCG}}
Net Investment Income Tax (3.8%){{niit}}
State income tax ({{stateTaxRate}}){{stateTax}}
Total tax liability{{totalTaxThirdParty}}
Net after-tax proceeds{{netProceedsThirdParty}}
§ 04Scenario B: Sale to ESOP with §1042 Election

If the seller sells to an ESOP and makes the §1042 election, reinvesting 100% of proceeds in Qualified Replacement Securities:

ItemAmount
Gross sale proceeds{{salePrice}}
Capital gain deferred (§1042){{capitalGain}}
Federal LTCG tax at closing$0
NIIT at closing$0
State tax at closing{{stateTaxESOP}}
Total tax at closing{{totalTaxESOP}}
Net proceeds at closing{{netProceedsESOP}}
Deferred gain (taxed when QRS sold){{capitalGain}}
State tax note: {{stateTaxNote}}
§ 05Side-by-Side Comparison
MetricThird-Party SaleESOP + §1042
Tax at closing{{totalTaxThirdParty}}{{totalTaxESOP}}
Net at closing{{netProceedsThirdParty}}{{netProceedsESOP}}
Tax savings at closing—{{taxSavings}}
Deferred gain$0{{capitalGain}}
Basis in QRSN/A{{qrsBasis}}
§ 06Additional Tax Considerations
A.Installment sale treatment. If part of the ESOP sale price is a seller note, the installment method may apply. The §1042 election interacts with installment sale rules — CPA should evaluate.
B.Entity conversion. If the company converts from LLC/S-Corp to C-Corp, the conversion may trigger built-in gains. CPA should model the conversion tax separately.
C.Estate tax. QRS held until death receive a stepped-up basis under §1014. The deferred gain may never be recognized if held long-term.
D.Charitable planning. Some sellers donate a portion of QRS to charity, receiving a deduction and avoiding the deferred gain. CPA and estate counsel should evaluate.
§ 07CPA Action Items

We request that the seller's CPA review the following:

1.Verify basis. Confirm the seller's adjusted basis in the company stock, including any prior adjustments, contributions, or distributions.
2.Verify state conformity. Confirm whether the seller's state conforms to §1042. If not, calculate the state tax impact at closing.
3.Model entity conversion. If applicable, model the tax impact of LLC/S-Corp to C-Corp conversion.
4.Confirm NIIT applicability. Verify that the Net Investment Income Tax applies at the 3.8% rate based on the seller's AGI.
5.Review QRS strategy. Discuss reinvestment options, estate planning implications, and charitable planning opportunities.
§ 08Contact
